title: API Gateway Pattern
summary: API gateway responsibilities, BFF pattern, Spring Cloud Gateway, rate limiting, authentication at the edge.

sections.content:
## What is an API Gateway?

An API Gateway is the **single entry point** for all clients. It handles cross-cutting concerns at the edge — auth, rate limiting, routing, SSL termination, request aggregation — so individual services don't need to.

Without a gateway:
```
Client knows about 10+ service URLs
Each service implements auth separately
Clients handle service discovery
CORS, SSL managed per service
```

With a gateway:
```
Client calls one URL
Gateway handles auth, routing, rate limiting, SSL
Services focus on business logic only
```

sections.title: API Gateway Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    Mobile[Mobile App] --> GW[API Gateway]
    Web[Web App] --> GW
    Partner[Partner API] --> GW
    GW -->|/api/orders| OS[Order Service]
    GW -->|/api/users| US[User Service]
    GW -->|/api/products| PS[Product Service]
    GW -->|/api/payments| PAY[Payment Service]
    GW --- Auth[Auth Filter]
    GW --- RL[Rate Limiter]
    GW --- LB[Load Balancer]
    GW --- Log[Access Log]
    style GW fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Spring Cloud Gateway

```yaml
# application.yml
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: order-service
          uri: lb://order-service     # load-balanced via Eureka/K8s
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/orders/**
          filters:
            - StripPrefix=1           # remove /api prefix
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: orderService
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/orders

        - id: user-service
          uri: lb://user-service
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/users/**
          filters:
            - AddRequestHeader=X-Gateway-Source, api-gateway
```

sections.title: Request Processing Pipeline in Spring Cloud Gateway
sections.content:
graph LR
    Req[Request] --> P[Predicate\nPath matching]
    P -->|match| F1[Pre-filters\nAuth, Rate limit, Headers]
    F1 --> Proxy[Proxy to service]
    Proxy --> F2[Post-filters\nResponse transform, Headers]
    F2 --> Resp[Response]
    P -->|no match| 404[404 Not Found]
    style P fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.question: What problem does the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern solve?
options: It reduces the number of microservices needed
It provides client-specific APIs optimized for each client type (mobile, web, partner) instead of one generic API for all
It moves backend logic to the frontend to reduce server load
It provides a fallback for when backend services are unavailable
sections.explanation: A single generic API must satisfy all clients, leading to over-fetching for some and multiple round-trips for others. BFF creates a dedicated gateway per client type. The mobile BFF returns small payloads optimized for bandwidth. The web BFF aggregates richer data. Each BFF is owned by the team that owns the frontend.
